_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2024 NIA-AA Revised Criteria (Jack et al), CLARITY-AD (van Dyck PMID 36449413), TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 (Sims PMID 37459141 / Mintun PMID 33720637), Lancet Commission 2024 (Livingston PMID 39096926), 2024 Alzheimer's Association Facts and Figures, and WHO 2023 global dementia data.

h1Title: Alzheimer's Disease: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia, driven by extracellular beta-amyloid plaques and intracellular hyperphosphorylated tau tangles that destroy neurons in the medial temporal lobe and spread through the cortex over 15-25 years. It accounts for 60-70% of the 55 million people living with dementia worldwide and affects approximately 7 million adults in the United States, with prevalence doubling every five years after age 65. The earliest reliable sign is progressive short-term memory loss — forgetting recent conversations, appointments, and recently learned facts — followed by language, executive, and visuospatial decline. Diagnosis now uses biomarkers (CSF, plasma p-tau217, amyloid PET) rather than waiting for severe symptoms. Two anti-amyloid antibodies, lecanemab and donanemab, can slow decline by 27-35% in early disease.

keyStats.prevalence: ~7 million US adults age 65+ (Alzheimer's Association 2024); 1 in 9 over 65
keyStats.demographics: Women account for ~two-thirds of US cases; Black and Hispanic adults have 1.5-2x higher prevalence than White adults
keyStats.avgAge: Late-onset typically begins after age 65; early-onset (<65) is 5-10% of cases
keyStats.globalCases: ~33-39 million globally with AD (60-70% of 55M dementia cases, WHO 2023)

definition: Alzheimer's disease (ICD-10: G30) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder defined by two co-existing pathologies: extracellular plaques of misfolded amyloid-beta peptide (primarily Aβ42) and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles of hyperphosphorylated tau protein. These changes begin 15-25 years before symptoms appear, first in the entorhinal cortex and hippocampus, then spreading through the neocortex along a stereotyped Braak staging pattern. Synaptic dysfunction, neuroinflammation driven by reactive microglia, and bulk neuronal loss follow, producing the clinical syndrome of progressive amnestic dementia. The 2024 National Institute on Aging-Alzheimer's Association (NIA-AA) Revised Criteria reclassified Alzheimer's as a biological diagnosis defined by core biomarkers — Aβ42/40 ratio, phosphorylated tau (p-tau181, p-tau217), and downstream neurodegeneration markers — rather than purely clinical syndrome. The framework expanded from AT(N) to ATX(N), incorporating inflammation, vascular, and synaptic markers. Late-onset Alzheimer's disease (after age 65) is the most common form; rare autosomal-dominant early-onset disease is caused by mutations in APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2. Neurologists, geriatricians, and behavioral neurologists manage the condition, often with psychiatry, social work, and palliative care involvement as disease progresses.

typesClassification.description: Biomarker-positive (amyloid PET or CSF Aβ42/40 abnormal) but no measurable cognitive deficit. May last 10-20 years. Identified mainly in research cohorts and through plasma p-tau217 screening.

typesClassification.type: Mild cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's
typesClassification.description: Objective cognitive deficit (typically memory) detectable on testing but daily function preserved. Annual conversion to dementia is 10-15%. Now the primary window for disease-modifying therapy.
Cognitive impairment interferes with complex tasks (finances, driving, work) but basic self-care is intact. MMSE typically 21-26; CDR global score 1.
Help required with basic activities of daily living. Pronounced language, executive, and visuospatial decline. Behavioral and psychological symptoms (BPSD) commonly emerge. MMSE 10-20; CDR 2.
Total dependence for self-care, loss of meaningful verbal communication, immobility, dysphagia, incontinence. Median time from diagnosis to severe stage is 4-7 years. MMSE under 10; CDR 3.

Onset before age 65, often in 40s-50s, caused by pathogenic variants in APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2. Each child of an affected parent has 50% risk. Course is similar but more rapid; atypical presentations (posterior cortical atrophy, logopenic aphasia) are over-represented.

primarySymptoms: Progressive short-term memory loss — forgetting recent conversations, repeating questions within minutes, misplacing items, and missing appointments — is the earliest and most reliable symptom in 80-90% of typical cases.
Word-finding difficulty with hesitations, circumlocutions ('the thing you write with'), and substitutions; reading and reading comprehension decline later than speech production.
Executive dysfunction: trouble planning meals, managing finances, following multi-step instructions, and adapting to new situations. Often noticed first by family during travel or financial tasks.
Visuospatial impairment producing getting lost in familiar places, difficulty judging distances when driving, and trouble using familiar tools — sometimes mistaken for vision loss.
Disorientation to time and place, beginning with date confusion and progressing to inability to identify season, year, or the home address.
Loss of insight into the deficit (anosognosia) — patients often deny memory problems even when objective testing is clearly abnormal. Family members are usually a more reliable source of history.
Apraxia — difficulty performing learned motor sequences such as dressing, using cutlery, or operating familiar appliances despite preserved motor strength.

Behavioral and psychological symptoms (BPSD): apathy in over 70% of patients, depression in 40-50%, anxiety, irritability, paranoid ideation, agitation, and night-time wandering as disease progresses.
Aphasia and agnosia in moderate stages — failure to recognise faces of relatives, name common objects, or understand complex sentences.
Incontinence, immobility, dysphagia, and mutism in severe stages, ultimately rendering the patient bedbound and dependent for all care.
earlyWarningSigns: Repeated short-term memory lapses noticed by family members that the patient denies or minimises
New difficulty handling personal finances, taxes, or online banking that the person previously managed without help
Subtle word-finding pauses and reliance on filler phrases such as 'that thing' or 'whatchamacallit' in a previously articulate person
Getting briefly lost driving familiar routes or missing a familiar exit
Withdrawal from hobbies, social events, or conversations that require following multiple speakers
emergencySigns: Sudden severe confusion or rapid worsening over hours to days — suggests delirium from infection, medication, metabolic derangement, or stroke, not Alzheimer's progression
First-ever seizure in a patient with known or suspected dementia — warrants brain imaging and neurology review
Sudden focal weakness, slurred speech, or facial droop — stroke until proven otherwise, regardless of background cognitive impairment
Headache, vomiting, or new gait disturbance with cognitive decline — rule out subdural haematoma, hydrocephalus, or mass lesion with urgent CT or MRI
Severe agitation with risk of harm to self or carer — emergency assessment for delirium, pain, infection, or medication adverse effect before antipsychotics are added

causes.description: Cleavage of amyloid precursor protein (APP) by beta- and gamma-secretases produces amyloid-beta peptides, particularly Aβ42, that misfold and aggregate into extracellular plaques. Plaques begin in the neocortex 15-25 years before symptoms and disrupt synaptic function, inflame surrounding microglia, and seed downstream tau pathology.

Tau, a microtubule-stabilising protein, becomes abnormally phosphorylated, detaches from microtubules, and aggregates into intracellular neurofibrillary tangles. Tangles begin in the entorhinal cortex and hippocampus and spread cortically along the Braak staging pattern. Tau burden correlates more closely with cognitive decline than amyloid burden.

Soluble Aβ oligomers and tau drive failure of glutamatergic and cholinergic synapses, the latter explaining the rationale for cholinesterase inhibitor therapy. Bulk neuronal loss follows, producing the medial temporal and parietal atrophy visible on MRI.

Reactive microglia and astrocytes around plaques release pro-inflammatory cytokines that amplify neuronal injury. Genetic risk variants in TREM2, CR1, and CLU implicate innate immunity in disease initiation.

causes.cause: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy and vascular contribution
causes.description: Amyloid-beta also deposits in cerebral vessel walls, causing microbleeds and lobar haemorrhage. Mixed Alzheimer's and vascular pathology accounts for the majority of dementia cases in older adults at autopsy.

causes.cause: Genetic mutations in APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2 (autosomal dominant early-onset disease)
causes.description: Pathogenic variants in any of these three genes cause overproduction of Aβ42 and produce highly penetrant early-onset Alzheimer's disease, typically with onset in the 30s-50s. Each child of an affected parent has 50% risk.

The APOE-ε4 variant on chromosome 19 is the strongest common genetic risk factor for late-onset disease. ε4 reduces clearance of Aβ from the brain. One copy raises lifetime risk roughly 3-fold; two copies raise it 9-15 fold.

riskFactors.description: The strongest risk factor by far. Prevalence is ~3% at age 65-74, ~17% at 75-84, and ~32% at 85+ (Alzheimer's Association 2024). Most cases occur in those over 75.
One APOE-ε4 allele raises lifetime risk ~3x; two copies raise risk 9-15x and lower mean onset age by 10-15 years. Autosomal dominant APP, PSEN1, PSEN2 mutations confer near-100% risk.

Women account for roughly two-thirds of US Alzheimer's cases. Part of the difference reflects greater life expectancy, but biological factors (oestrogen withdrawal, APOE-ε4 interaction) may also contribute.
Systolic blood pressure ≥140 mmHg in midlife increases late-life dementia risk by 60%. SPRINT-MIND showed intensive blood pressure control reduced MCI incidence by 19% (PMID 30688979).
Diabetes raises Alzheimer's risk roughly 1.5-2x. Brain insulin resistance impairs Aβ clearance and energy metabolism; midlife glycaemic control reduces late-life dementia risk.

Untreated hearing loss approximately doubles dementia risk. The 2024 Lancet Commission identifies hearing loss as the largest single modifiable contributor (~7% of population-attributable risk). Hearing aids reduce risk in at-risk populations (ACHIEVE trial 2023).

riskFactors.factor: Low educational attainment and cognitive reserve
riskFactors.description: Fewer than 12 years of formal education raises dementia risk roughly 1.6x. Higher education and lifelong cognitive engagement build neural reserve that delays clinical onset even at equivalent brain pathology.

Each independently raises dementia risk by 30-60%. Mid-life obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and current smoking together account for 10-15% of population-attributable dementia risk per Lancet Commission.
Mid- and late-life depression raises dementia risk roughly 1.9x and may be both a prodrome and a contributor. Social isolation independently raises risk by ~25%.

Moderate-to-severe TBI raises lifetime dementia risk 1.5-4x. Repetitive mild head injury (boxing, American football) is linked to chronic traumatic encephalopathy, which overlaps clinically with Alzheimer's.
More than 21 units per week raises dementia risk; heavy drinking (>14 units/week) is linked to alcohol-related brain damage that can coexist with Alzheimer's pathology.

Long-term exposure to fine particulate matter (PM2.5) raises dementia risk roughly 1.1x per 2 µg/m³ increase; the Lancet Commission classifies air pollution as a modifiable contributor representing ~3% of population risk.

riskFactors.factor: Untreated vision loss and high LDL cholesterol
riskFactors.description: Added to the Lancet Commission 2024 list of 14 modifiable risk factors. Cataract surgery is associated with reduced dementia incidence; LDL ≥4.4 mmol/L in midlife raises late-life dementia risk.

affectedDemographics: ~7 million US adults aged 65+ live with Alzheimer's; this is projected to reach 13.8 million by 2060 (Alzheimer's Association 2024)
Women make up ~two-thirds of US Alzheimer's cases, partly reflecting longer life expectancy

Black adults are about twice as likely, and Hispanic adults about 1.5x as likely, to have Alzheimer's compared with non-Hispanic White adults, driven by differences in vascular risk and social determinants
Globally an estimated 33-39 million people live with Alzheimer's; low- and middle-income countries already account for over 60% of cases (WHO 2023)
Early-onset disease (under age 65) is uncommon — roughly 5-10% of all cases — but disproportionately affects working-age adults and their families
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows a stepwise workflow combining clinical assessment, structured cognitive testing, screening laboratory studies, structural brain imaging, and increasingly, fluid and PET biomarkers. The decisive history is taken from both the patient and a knowledgeable informant — the informant usually provides the most accurate picture of functional change. Structured cognitive testing with the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) or Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) quantifies global cognition; the MoCA is more sensitive for mild cognitive impairment. A standard work-up screens for treatable mimics with TSH, vitamin B12, comprehensive metabolic panel, depression assessment, and brain imaging (MRI preferred over CT) to rule out subdural haematoma, normal-pressure hydrocephalus, vascular disease, and structural lesions. MRI in Alzheimer's typically shows medial temporal lobe atrophy with relatively preserved frontal and motor regions. The 2024 NIA-AA Revised Criteria established Alzheimer's as a biological diagnosis defined by core biomarkers. Cerebrospinal fluid testing for the Aβ42/40 ratio plus phosphorylated tau (p-tau181 or p-tau217) is sensitive and specific; amyloid PET imaging with florbetapir, florbetaben, or flutemetamol can confirm cerebral amyloid in vivo. Tau PET is now used to stage disease. Plasma p-tau217 assays have emerged as a high-accuracy blood test (positive and negative predictive values exceeding 90% in specialist clinics) and are being introduced as a first-line biomarker. Differential diagnosis must exclude vascular dementia, dementia with Lewy bodies, frontotemporal dementia, depression-related pseudodementia, and normal-pressure hydrocephalus — each has distinct features and management implications.

diagnosticTests.test: Structured cognitive testing (MoCA, MMSE) with informant history
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies severity of cognitive impairment, establishes baseline for tracking, and identifies the cognitive domains affected. The MoCA is more sensitive than MMSE for mild cognitive impairment and early Alzheimer's.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute structured interview in the clinic covering memory, attention, language, visuospatial skills, and executive function. A relative or close friend is interviewed separately about daily-function changes.

diagnosticTests.test: Screening blood panel (CBC, CMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes reversible contributors to cognitive decline — hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, hepatic or renal encephalopathy, anaemia. Recommended in every dementia work-up by AAN and NICE.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard fasting or non-fasting blood draw. Results return within 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI (or CT if MRI contraindicated)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies medial temporal lobe atrophy supporting Alzheimer's; rules out vascular dementia, normal-pressure hydrocephalus, subdural haematoma, tumours, and other structural causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan inside the MRI tube; non-contrast protocols are standard. Mild claustrophobia is common; let the radiographer know in advance.

diagnosticTests.test: Cerebrospinal fluid Aβ42/40 ratio and phosphorylated tau (p-tau181 or p-tau217)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Core biomarker confirmation per the 2024 NIA-AA Revised Criteria. A low Aβ42/40 ratio plus elevated p-tau is highly specific for Alzheimer's pathology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture performed in clinic or day-care unit; the procedure takes 20-30 minutes. Patients rest flat afterwards; post-LP headache occurs in 10-20% and usually settles within 48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Emerging blood-based biomarker with diagnostic accuracy comparable to CSF and PET in specialist settings; expected to become the first-line screening test before confirmatory CSF or PET.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw sent to a specialised laboratory. Results typically return within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Amyloid PET imaging (florbetapir, florbetaben, or flutemetamol)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises cerebral amyloid plaques in vivo. Required for confirming amyloid positivity before initiating anti-amyloid antibody therapy (lecanemab, donanemab).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radioactive tracer is injected, followed by a 30-minute wait and a 15-20 minute PET scan. Total appointment time is 60-90 minutes. Radiation dose is comparable to a CT scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises tau tangle distribution and burden; useful for staging disease and assessing response to therapy. Predicts rate of clinical decline more accurately than amyloid PET.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Similar to amyloid PET — tracer injection, wait, and a 20-30 minute scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies APOE-ε4 homozygotes who carry markedly higher risk of amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA) on anti-amyloid therapy; informs treatment risk-benefit discussion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood or buccal sample. Results take 1-2 weeks and should be discussed with a clinician or genetic counsellor before testing.

treatmentOverview: Alzheimer's treatment now has three tracks: symptomatic medications, disease-modifying anti-amyloid antibodies, and non-pharmacological care. Symptomatic therapy with cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) modestly improves cognition, function, and behaviour by raising synaptic acetylcholine. Memantine, an NMDA-receptor antagonist, is added in moderate-to-severe disease. Effect sizes are modest — typically a 6-12 month delay in functional decline — but consistent across trials. The biggest change in the past three years has been the FDA approval of two monoclonal antibodies that clear cerebral amyloid plaques. Lecanemab (Leqembi) received traditional FDA approval in July 2023 on the basis of the CLARITY-AD trial, which showed a 27% slowing of the Clinical Dementia Rating–Sum of Boxes decline over 18 months. Donanemab (Kisunla) received traditional approval in July 2024 after the TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 trial showed a 35% slowing of the integrated Alzheimer's Disease Rating Scale (iADRS) decline. Both are reserved for biomarker-confirmed early Alzheimer's (MCI or mild dementia) and require regular MRI surveillance for amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-E oedema and ARIA-H microhaemorrhages), which are 2-3 times more common in APOE-ε4 homozygotes. Aducanumab, the first agent in this class, was withdrawn from the market in 2024 after the manufacturer discontinued the program. Brexpiprazole received FDA approval in 2023 for Alzheimer's-related agitation, the first labelled indication for this symptom; antipsychotics broadly should still be avoided where possible because of mortality risk. Non-pharmacological care — caregiver education, structured daily routines, dementia-friendly environments, advance care planning, and management of depression, sleep, and pain — is at least as important as drugs across the disease course.

medicalTreatments.name: Donepezil (5 mg daily, titrated to 10 mg after 4-6 weeks; 23 mg available for moderate-severe disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Cholinesterase inhibitor; raises synaptic acetylcholine in cortex and hippocampus. First-line symptomatic therapy across all stages from mild to severe. Common side effects are nausea, diarrhoea, vivid dreams, bradycardia, and muscle cramps.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest cognitive benefit of 2-3 points on the ADAS-Cog scale over 6 months; delays nursing home placement by 4-6 months in meta-analyses. About 25-50% of patients show a clinically meaningful response.

medicalTreatments.name: Rivastigmine (oral 1.5-6 mg twice daily; transdermal patch 4.6, 9.5, or 13.3 mg/24h)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits both acetylcholinesterase and butyrylcholinesterase. The transdermal patch substantially reduces gastrointestinal side effects compared with oral therapy and is often preferred when nausea limits dosing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar magnitude of effect to donepezil; the 13.3 mg/24h patch is approved for severe disease and slows functional decline by ~25% over 6 months versus 9.5 mg/24h.

medicalTreatments.name: Galantamine (extended-release 8-24 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Cholinesterase inhibitor with additional allosteric modulation of nicotinic receptors. Equivalent to donepezil and rivastigmine in head-to-head trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ADAS-Cog improvement of ~3 points at 6 months; meaningful global response in roughly 30% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Memantine (start 5 mg daily, titrate to 10 mg twice daily over 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Uncompetitive NMDA-receptor antagonist that reduces glutamate excitotoxicity. Approved for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease; often combined with a cholinesterase inhibitor in this stage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit on cognition, function, and behaviour; combination with donepezil reduces clinical worsening over 24 weeks by 10-20% versus donepezil alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Lecanemab (Leqembi, 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Humanised IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting soluble Aβ protofibrils; clears cerebral amyloid plaques. FDA traditional approval July 2023. Required: biomarker-confirmed amyloid positivity, MCI or mild dementia stage, baseline and surveillance MRI for ARIA, APOE genotyping for risk stratification.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CLARITY-AD trial: 27% slowing of CDR-SB decline over 18 months versus placebo (van Dyck NEJM 2023, PMID 36449413). ARIA-E in ~13% (most asymptomatic); higher in APOE-ε4 homozygotes.

medicalTreatments.name: Donanemab (Kisunla, 700 mg IV every 4 weeks for first 3 doses, then 1400 mg every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Humanised monoclonal antibody targeting an N-terminal pyroglutamate epitope of amyloid plaques. FDA traditional approval July 2024. Infusion stops once amyloid PET is plaque-clear. Surveillance MRI for ARIA required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 trial: 35% slowing of iADRS decline over 76 weeks in low/medium-tau patients (Sims JAMA 2023, PMID 37459141). ARIA-E in ~24% overall, higher in APOE-ε4 homozygotes (~41%).

medicalTreatments.name: Brexpiprazole (0.5-3 mg daily, titrated over 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Atypical antipsychotic; first FDA-approved (2023) for Alzheimer's-related agitation. Reserved for moderate-severe agitation when non-pharmacological measures fail. Carries class warning for increased mortality in dementia-related psychosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory score by ~5 points versus placebo at 12 weeks; numbers needed to treat ~4-8 for clinically meaningful improvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Citalopram or sertraline (for depression and agitation)
medicalTreatments.description: SSRIs treat coexisting depression in 40-50% of patients and have modest evidence for reducing agitation. Citalopram is dose-capped at 20 mg daily over age 60 due to QT prolongation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Depression response rates of 40-60%; CitAD trial showed modest agitation benefit, though QT concerns limit dose.

surgicalOptions.name: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt for coexisting normal-pressure hydrocephalus
surgicalOptions.description: Selected patients with overlapping idiopathic normal-pressure hydrocephalus (gait, urinary, and cognitive disturbance with enlarged ventricles) may benefit from shunting. Pre-operative tap test response predicts surgical benefit.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Gait improvement in 60-70% of well-selected NPH cases; cognitive benefit is more modest, especially when Alzheimer's pathology coexists.

surgicalOptions.name: Cataract surgery as cognitive risk reduction
surgicalOptions.description: Restores vision and may slow cognitive decline in patients with significant cataract — observational data show ~30% reduction in incident dementia after cataract extraction. Not a treatment for established Alzheimer's but valuable in coexisting visual impairment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Standard cataract surgery success rate of 95%+; cognitive benefit applies primarily to prevention rather than reversal.

alternativeTreatments.name: FINGER-style multidomain lifestyle intervention
alternativeTreatments.description: Combined diet, exercise, cognitive training, and vascular risk management slows cognitive decline in at-risk older adults (FINGER trial, Ngandu Lancet 2015 PMID 25771249). Recommended adjunctively for all early-stage patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive stimulation therapy and structured cognitive rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Group-based programmes show small but consistent benefits in cognition and quality of life in mild-to-moderate disease; NICE NG97 recommends offering it.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aerobic and resistance exercise (150 minutes/week)
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest cognitive benefits in MCI and mild dementia; reduces falls, depression, and overall mortality. Evidence is strongest for combined aerobic plus resistance training.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a neurologist, geriatrician, or memory clinic when cognitive symptoms persist beyond 6 months, atypical features are present (early language or visuospatial decline, age under 65, rapid progression, prominent psychiatric symptoms), when biomarker confirmation is needed before disease-modifying therapy, when imaging suggests alternative diagnoses, or when behavioural symptoms are severe. Primary care can monitor stable, established disease, but specialist input is essential for diagnosis, treatment initiation, and complex symptom management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a behavioural neurologist or geriatrician with memory-clinic experience, access to biomarker testing (CSF, plasma p-tau, amyloid PET), and an established pathway for anti-amyloid antibody therapy where appropriate. Ask whether the practice screens for APOE genotype before disease-modifying therapy and how ARIA surveillance is managed. Continuity matters — Alzheimer's care is a multi-year relationship spanning diagnosis, treatment, behavioural complications, and end-of-life planning.
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure to under 130/80 mmHg in mid-life and early late-life — SPRINT-MIND showed a 19% reduction in MCI incidence with intensive control
Treat hearing loss with appropriately fitted hearing aids — the ACHIEVE trial demonstrated 48% slowing of cognitive decline in at-risk older adults
Maintain at least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity plus twice-weekly resistance training to reduce dementia risk by 30-40%
Manage type 2 diabetes, LDL cholesterol, and avoid smoking — combined modifiable factors account for ~45% of population-attributable dementia risk (Lancet Commission 2024)
Stay cognitively and socially engaged — continued learning, social activity, and a varied occupation build cognitive reserve that delays clinical onset
Limit alcohol to under 14 units per week and treat depression promptly when it appears
lifestyleModifications: Establish a predictable daily routine — fixed wake, meal, activity, and sleep times reduce confusion and behavioural symptoms
Simplify the home environment with clear signage, removed trip hazards, locked-out hot taps and stove controls, and a single accessible toilet
Use a single calendar, whiteboard, or smartphone reminder system shared with carers to track appointments and medication
Maintain regular physical exercise — walking, swimming, or chair-based exercise 5 days a week — adapted to ability
Engage in social and cognitive activities the patient enjoys (music, reading aloud, light gardening, family meals) at intensities matched to current ability
Prioritise sleep hygiene — consistent bedtime, daylight exposure, and treatment of sleep apnoea if present
Plan financial, legal, and healthcare decisions early — lasting power of attorney, advance directives, and care preferences are easier to capture in mild stages

recommended: Mediterranean or MIND (Mediterranean-DASH Intervention for Neurodegenerative Delay) eating pattern — observational reduction in incident Alzheimer's of 35-53% in highest adherence groups
Leafy green vegetables daily — spinach, kale, lettuce; one serving daily linked to slower cognitive decline
Berries 2-3 times weekly — blueberries and strawberries; associated with delayed cognitive ageing
Olive oil as the primary cooking fat; fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) at least twice weekly
Nuts, whole grains, beans, and poultry over red meat
Adequate hydration — older adults are prone to chronic dehydration that worsens confusion
avoid: Heavy intake of processed and red meat, fried foods, pastries, and sweets — linked to faster cognitive decline
Sugar-sweetened beverages and ultra-processed foods high in added sugar and sodium
Excessive alcohol — keep within 14 units/week or less; binge drinking accelerates cognitive loss
Saturated and trans fats from butter, margarine, and processed foods in large amounts
Crash diets and prolonged calorie restriction — increase frailty risk and may worsen cognition in late-stage disease
Anticholinergic-heavy supplement regimens — many over-the-counter sleep, allergy, and bladder products carry anticholinergic burden that worsens cognition

exerciseGuidelines: Aim for 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity (walking, cycling, swimming) plus twice-weekly resistance training and balance work. In moderate dementia, supervised group programmes or chair-based routines are safer. Falls prevention becomes a priority as the disease advances — physiotherapy review for gait, footwear, and home hazards is recommended at least annually.
dailyManagement: Set medications in a weekly pill organiser and link doses to fixed daily events (breakfast, lights-out) to support adherence
Carry an ID card or wear a medical bracelet noting the diagnosis, emergency contact, and current medications
Schedule regular check-ins with the primary care team for blood pressure, diabetes, vision, hearing, dental, and foot care
Track behaviour, sleep, and any new symptoms in a simple diary — patterns help clinicians distinguish disease progression from delirium
Plan respite for the primary carer at least monthly; carer burnout drives premature nursing home admission
Review medication list at least annually for anticholinergic burden and unnecessary sedatives
prognosis: Median survival from clinical diagnosis of Alzheimer's dementia is 8-10 years, with substantial variation depending on age at diagnosis, sex, comorbidities, and disease stage. Patients diagnosed in their late 60s often live 10-15 years; those diagnosed after 80 typically 4-6 years. Progression follows a relatively predictable trajectory: 2-4 years from MCI to mild dementia, 4-7 years through moderate stage, and 1-3 years in severe dementia. Death is most often from pneumonia, urinary tract infection with sepsis, malnutrition, pressure injuries, or pulmonary embolism rather than the dementia itself. Anti-amyloid antibody therapy (lecanemab, donanemab) slows decline by 25-35% over 18-24 months in early disease; longer-term effects are still being characterised. Prognosis is improved by treating coexisting vascular risk factors, depression, sleep apnoea, hearing loss, and pain. Predictors of faster decline include APOE-ε4 homozygosity, higher tau burden on PET, early age at onset, prominent neuropsychiatric symptoms, and significant comorbid vascular disease.
recoveryTimeline: Alzheimer's is progressive and not reversible with current therapy, so 'recovery' applies to the timeline of disease control rather than cure. Cholinesterase inhibitors typically deliver their measurable benefit within 3-6 months of titration; about 25-50% of patients show a clear response. Anti-amyloid antibody therapy clears amyloid plaques within 12-18 months in most responders, often allowing donanemab to be stopped once PET is plaque-clear. ARIA, if it occurs, is usually within the first 6 months of therapy and typically resolves over 4-12 weeks after pausing or discontinuing treatment. Functional decline continues despite therapy — at a slowed rate — across all stages.
complications: Pneumonia and aspiration — the leading cause of death; risk rises sharply once dysphagia develops in moderate-severe disease
Recurrent urinary tract infections and sepsis, often complicated by delirium that may be mistaken for disease progression
Falls and hip fractures — annual fall rate exceeds 30% in moderate dementia; fractures carry high mortality in this group
Pressure injuries, malnutrition, and dehydration in late-stage disease, particularly in the bedbound phase
Behavioural and psychological symptoms (BPSD) — agitation, psychosis, aggression — driving carer burnout, hospitalisation, and antipsychotic prescribing
Amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA-E and ARIA-H) on anti-amyloid antibody therapy — usually asymptomatic but occasionally severe; risk is highest in APOE-ε4 homozygotes
Carer depression, anxiety, and physical illness — affects more than 40% of family carers and is a major outcome in its own right

supportResources.name: Alzheimer's Association (US) — Helpline and Resources
supportResources.description: 24/7 helpline (1-800-272-3900), local support groups, care navigation, and patient education.

supportResources.name: Alzheimer's Society (UK)
supportResources.description: UK national charity with helpline, online forum, dementia advisers, and clinical guideline summaries.

supportResources.name: National Institute on Aging — Alzheimer's and Related Dementias
supportResources.description: US federal information hub with evidence summaries, clinical trial listings, and caregiver resources.

supportResources.name: Alzheimer's Disease International
supportResources.description: Global federation of Alzheimer's associations; annual World Alzheimer Report and country-level statistics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vascular Dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vascular dementia typically begins more abruptly after a stroke or progresses in stepwise jumps, with prominent executive and gait deficits, focal neurological signs, and extensive white matter or infarct burden on MRI. Memory is relatively preserved early. Amyloid biomarkers are negative, distinguishing it from Alzheimer's, though mixed pathology is common in older adults.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dementia with Lewy Bodies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lewy body dementia features early fluctuating cognition, well-formed visual hallucinations, REM sleep behaviour disorder, and parkinsonism (rigidity, bradykinesia) within a year of cognitive symptoms. Severe sensitivity to typical antipsychotics is a red flag. DAT-SPECT shows reduced striatal dopamine uptake. Alzheimer's lacks these features and shows amnestic, not visuospatial-and-attention, onset.

confusedWithConditions.name: Frontotemporal Dementia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frontotemporal dementia typically begins before age 65 with behavioural change (disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive behaviours) or progressive aphasia, not amnesia. MRI shows frontal or anterior temporal atrophy rather than medial temporal predominance. Amyloid biomarkers are negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mild Cognitive Impairment
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MCI is a syndrome of objective cognitive decline without functional impairment. Amnestic MCI with positive Alzheimer's biomarkers is preclinical/prodromal Alzheimer's; non-amnestic and biomarker-negative MCI has many other causes. Daily function being preserved is the defining boundary from dementia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Normal-Pressure Hydrocephalus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NPH classically presents with the triad of magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and subcortical cognitive slowing. MRI shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy. A high-volume tap test improving gait predicts shunt response. Memory deficit is mild and improves with shunting — unlike Alzheimer's.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression (Pseudodementia)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression in older adults can mimic dementia with poor concentration, slowed thought, and memory complaints. Unlike Alzheimer's, depressed patients usually emphasise their cognitive failures, perform inconsistently on testing, and improve with antidepressant treatment. The two often coexist — depression in mid-life is itself a dementia risk factor.

relatedConditions.name: Mild Cognitive Impairment
relatedConditions.relevance: MCI is the prodromal stage of Alzheimer's in biomarker-positive patients; annual conversion to dementia is 10-15%, and it is the primary window for disease-modifying therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Vascular Dementia
relatedConditions.relevance: Mixed Alzheimer's-vascular pathology is the most common dementia substrate at autopsy in older adults; shared vascular risk factors are central to prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Dementia with Lewy Bodies
relatedConditions.relevance: Second most common neurodegenerative dementia; frequently misdiagnosed as Alzheimer's. Distinct treatment implications including extreme antipsychotic sensitivity.

relatedConditions.name: Frontotemporal Dementia
relatedConditions.relevance: Younger-onset neurodegenerative dementia in the same differential diagnosis; behavioural and language presentations distinguish it from Alzheimer's.

relatedConditions.name: Parkinson's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Up to 80% of Parkinson's patients develop dementia after 10-20 years; alpha-synuclein and amyloid pathologies frequently overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Mid- and late-life depression is both a risk factor for and an early symptom of Alzheimer's; coexists in 40-50% of patients.

faqs.question: Is Alzheimer's disease the same as dementia?
faqs.answer: No. Dementia is the broader syndrome of progressive cognitive decline severe enough to impair daily function. Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia, accounting for 60-70% of cases worldwide. Other causes include vascular dementia, dementia with Lewy bodies, and frontotemporal dementia. Distinguishing them matters because treatment, prognosis, and family counselling differ.

faqs.question: What is the first sign of Alzheimer's disease?
faqs.answer: Progressive short-term memory loss is the earliest reliable sign in 80-90% of typical cases. Family members usually notice the change before the patient does, and the patient may deny or minimise the deficit. Word-finding difficulty and trouble managing finances often appear alongside memory change.

faqs.question: How is Alzheimer's disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines history from the patient and a relative, structured cognitive testing (MoCA or MMSE), basic blood work to rule out reversible causes, and brain MRI. Confirmation now uses biomarkers — CSF Aβ42/40 ratio with phosphorylated tau, plasma p-tau217 blood tests, or amyloid PET imaging.

faqs.question: Can a blood test diagnose Alzheimer's?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specialist settings. Plasma p-tau217 is a newer blood biomarker with diagnostic accuracy comparable to cerebrospinal fluid testing and amyloid PET in research and memory-clinic cohorts. Positive results are typically confirmed with CSF or PET before starting disease-modifying therapy.

faqs.question: What causes Alzheimer's disease?
faqs.answer: Two pathologies are central: extracellular plaques of beta-amyloid peptide and intracellular tangles of hyperphosphorylated tau, which together produce synaptic loss, neuroinflammation, and neuronal death over 15-25 years. Genetic factors (APOE-ε4 most importantly) interact with vascular and lifestyle risk factors to determine when symptoms appear.

faqs.question: Is Alzheimer's disease hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most Alzheimer's is not directly inherited but has a strong heritable component. The APOE-ε4 allele raises risk roughly 3x with one copy and 9-15x with two copies. Rare autosomal-dominant mutations in APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2 cause early-onset disease with near-100% penetrance; each child of an affected parent has 50% risk.

faqs.question: Can Alzheimer's disease be prevented?
faqs.answer: It cannot be guaranteed prevented, but roughly 45% of dementia cases worldwide are linked to 14 modifiable risk factors per the Lancet Commission 2024. Treating hypertension, hearing loss, depression, diabetes, and high LDL; staying physically and socially active; avoiding smoking; and limiting alcohol all measurably lower risk. Prevention is most effective when started in midlife.

faqs.question: What is lecanemab and how effective is it?
faqs.answer: Lecanemab (Leqembi) is a monoclonal antibody that clears beta-amyloid from the brain. It received traditional FDA approval in July 2023. In the CLARITY-AD trial it slowed cognitive and functional decline by 27% over 18 months in early Alzheimer's. It is given by IV infusion every 2 weeks and requires MRI surveillance for ARIA.

faqs.question: What is donanemab and how is it different from lecanemab?
faqs.answer: Donanemab (Kisunla) is a monoclonal antibody targeting an N-terminal pyroglutamate epitope of amyloid plaques. It received FDA traditional approval in July 2024. TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 showed a 35% slowing of decline over 76 weeks. Donanemab is dosed every 4 weeks and can be stopped once amyloid PET shows plaque clearance.

faqs.question: What is ARIA and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities) refers to MRI changes seen with anti-amyloid antibodies: ARIA-E (oedema) and ARIA-H (microhaemorrhages). Most cases are asymptomatic and detected only on surveillance MRI; a minority cause headache, confusion, or seizure. APOE-ε4 homozygotes have 2-3x higher rates.

faqs.question: Do cholinesterase inhibitors really work?
faqs.answer: Yes, modestly. Donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine produce small but consistent improvements in cognition, function, and behaviour averaging 2-3 points on the ADAS-Cog at 6 months, and delay nursing home placement by 4-6 months in meta-analyses. About 25-50% of patients show a clinically meaningful response.

faqs.question: How long do people live after an Alzheimer's diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Median survival from clinical diagnosis is 8-10 years, with wide variation. Patients diagnosed in their late 60s often live 10-15 years; those diagnosed after 80 typically 4-6 years. Death is most often from pneumonia, infection, or complications of immobility rather than the dementia itself.

faqs.question: What are the stages of Alzheimer's disease?
faqs.answer: Five stages are commonly recognised: preclinical (biomarker-positive, no symptoms), mild cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's (objective decline, function preserved), mild dementia (trouble with complex tasks), moderate dementia (help with basic care), and severe dementia (total dependence). Average progression from MCI to severe dementia is 8-12 years.

faqs.question: What is APOE-ε4 and should I get tested?
faqs.answer: APOE-ε4 is a variant on chromosome 19 that raises lifetime Alzheimer's risk roughly 3x with one copy and 9-15x with two copies. Testing is recommended before starting anti-amyloid antibody therapy because homozygotes have higher ARIA risk. Predictive testing in asymptomatic adults should be done with genetic counselling.

faqs.question: Can young people get Alzheimer's disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Early-onset Alzheimer's (under age 65) accounts for 5-10% of all cases. Most early-onset disease is sporadic, but a small minority is caused by autosomal-dominant mutations in APP, PSEN1, or PSEN2, often presenting in the 40s or 50s. Atypical presentations such as posterior cortical atrophy are over-represented.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Alzheimer's and Lewy body dementia?
faqs.answer: Lewy body dementia features early visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, REM sleep behaviour disorder, and parkinsonism appearing within a year of cognitive change. Severe sensitivity to typical antipsychotics is a hallmark. Alzheimer's begins with amnestic decline, lacks early hallucinations and parkinsonism, and shows medial temporal atrophy on MRI.

faqs.question: Can lifestyle changes slow Alzheimer's once diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Yes, modestly. Multidomain lifestyle programmes combining Mediterranean-style diet, regular exercise, cognitive engagement, social activity, and tight vascular risk control slow cognitive decline in at-risk older adults. Treating hearing loss, depression, sleep apnoea, and pain measurably improves cognition.

faqs.question: How much does anti-amyloid antibody therapy cost?
faqs.answer: Lecanemab costs approximately USD 26,500 per year for the drug alone in the United States; donanemab pricing is similar. Total annual cost including infusions, baseline and surveillance MRI scans, biomarker confirmation, and clinic visits typically runs USD 40,000-60,000. Medicare in the US covers therapy for biomarker-confirmed patients in approved registries.

faqs.question: Are antipsychotics safe in Alzheimer's-related agitation?
faqs.answer: Typical and atypical antipsychotics carry a class warning for increased mortality in older adults with dementia and should be avoided where possible. Brexpiprazole received FDA approval in 2023 specifically for Alzheimer's-related agitation when non-pharmacological measures fail. Citalopram and other SSRIs have modest evidence for agitation.

faqs.question: Why was aducanumab withdrawn?
faqs.answer: Aducanumab (Aduhelm) received controversial accelerated FDA approval in 2021 based on amyloid clearance rather than clinical benefit, with split trial data. Coverage restrictions, weak clinical evidence, and the arrival of lecanemab and donanemab — both with positive phase 3 trials — led the manufacturer Biogen to discontinue aducanumab in early 2024.

faqs.question: When should a person with Alzheimer's stop driving?
faqs.answer: Most patients with mild dementia can drive safely for a limited period, but skills decline within 1-3 years. Stop driving when there are getting-lost episodes, near-misses, decline on a formal driving assessment, or family concern about safety. Many jurisdictions require notification of a dementia diagnosis to the licensing authority.

faqs.question: What support is available for caregivers?
faqs.answer: National associations such as the Alzheimer's Association (US), Alzheimer's Society (UK), and Alzheimer's Disease International provide 24/7 helplines, online communities, local support groups, and care-navigation services. Practical respite, day-centre attendance, home aides, and structured caregiver education reduce burnout and delay nursing home admission.

simpleName: Alzheimer's Disease (most common form of dementia)
metaDescription: Alzheimer's disease: progressive memory loss from amyloid plaques and tau tangles. Symptoms, biomarker diagnosis, lecanemab, donanemab reviewed by MDs.
